turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument now in number oh oh oh, pardon me oh one three thirty-two, the Board of Education of Independent School District number ninety-two of Pottawatomie County versus Lindsay Earls. Ms. Meoli M- m- Meoli I guess. Is that is that the correct pronunciation?

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Meoli.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Meoli, okay. The third time is the charm. [Inaudible]

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. This case involves the constitutionality of the Tecumseh School Board's decision to implement a suspicionless drug testing pro- policy for students in competitive activities as a reasonable response to student drug use. Tecumseh's policy represents a natural, logical, and rational application of this Court's decision in Vernonia V Acton. Vernonia's policy applied to students who chose to participate in interscholastic athletics. Tecumseh's policy applies as well to athletics, but also to all the other competitive activities that are offered by the district. Respondents do not challenge the policy as applied to the athletics, conceding that this Court approved that practice in Vernonia. Competitive...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, this policy goes beyond what was permitted in Vernonia, does it not?

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: It greatly expands... Well, it covers...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: the number of students covered by the policy.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: It it covers a wider variety of interscholastic competitive activities.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: And the evidence is not as strong, i- is it, in this case of drug use in the school as as was the case in Vernonia?

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, we do not believe that is true.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, if you...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: go by the district court's findings, that's what you'd conclude.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: e- i- The district court in Vernonia?

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: In this case.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: In this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: What do you --

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: the young people who were involved in these particular extracurricular activities?

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Yes, we do have evidence of drug use w- i- with these students in extracurricular activities.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: In...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: of the the reports that have been filed up to the eve of the adoption of this regulation by the school district with the Feds, year after year after year, saying, things are fine here? The only thing we have to worry about is is some beer.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I don't think they were lying.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Federal grants...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: were they were saying at the same time that they didn't have a problem with with what we usually refer to as drugs.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: They said it e- it wasn't a major problem at that time.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And and in point of fact,

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: as I understand it, e- since the testing has been carried out among the the class of people subject to this challenge, there have been only three instances of of any drug use found.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: From the from the drug testing?

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: it seems to me that your evidentiary problem is up to the eve of adopting the regulation, the school district was saying, we don't have a problem.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Let me a- answer the second part first.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But the important piece...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: the existence of a policy might be expected to deter drug use so that you would have fewer i- instances after it was imposed.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Y- yes, Your Honor, and in and in...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: fact really it...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: know, will we?

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, l-

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: let her...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: answer the question, will you?

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Your Honor, in fact, it it really did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Ms. Meoli, d-

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: didn't we say in Vernonia e- that in Skinner, which was the th- railroad employee testing case, we did not demand that it be shown that the particular railroad had a drug problem? Didn't we say it was enough that there was a nationwide problem?

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: We...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: said that in...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Skinner, and we adverted to that in Vernonia.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: And i- and Vernonia's evidence as well showed that there was not a specific drug problem among athletes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Among...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: I thought the argument in Vernonia and I thought the opinion in Vernonia, in assessing the particular interest of the district, repeatedly emphasized the particular problems with the athletes.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: And I'm not saying that, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But isn't that the implication...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: of what you're...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: saying?

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I don't.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Except for one startling difference...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: The e- in e- n- there was a problem with athletes.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Well, e- e- Your Honor, we have never said that the the students in interscholastic competitive activities are the only students or even the most likely students to be abusing drugs.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Well, I'm...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: quotin-

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: I'm referring specifically to page one hundred and one hundred and one of the joint appendix where the question is whether the students who tend to be indevolved [: involved] in drugs are the ones most likely to be choosing extracurricular activities.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: That was the testimony of the of the board president, Dean Rogers.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Where is that testimony?

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: I think it is at page a hundred and six, Your Honor.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: We had no basis for saying yes or no.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Well, e- e- yes, he sa- he says that initially, and then he goes on to say, I just know that all age levels, all categories categories if we're categorizing the students that frequent the park.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Yes, but he's not...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: saying, as was the case in Vernon- Vernonia, that here is a group that we can identify that has this problem.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Y- your Honor, the use of ed- of of drugs among the students in competitive activities was just one of the factors as to why the Tecumseh board selected that group.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: So...

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: or or prevent parents from bring persecuted...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, if if you want...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: You don't You don't have to go that...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: far, do you, d- in...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: in order...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: to in order to justify limiting it to those who participate in in voluntary activities?

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It seems to me...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: you could say even if we could drug test anybody, we made the choice of giving a student who feels that strongly about not undergoing drug testing the ability to avoid it by simply not engaging in the extracurricular activities.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Yes, yes,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Your Honor. I...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: valid argument I I suppose an equally valid argument would i- for for testing everybody would be our object is to stop drug use, not to penalize people who go out for band.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: i- i- Your Honor, the i- the p- the purpose of this program is not to discipline students.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: those students...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: drug use among those who do not go out for band?

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, I I I...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: believe we do.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: good that you can test everybody?

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: W- well, number one, I I think there is a deterrent effect for implementing this program among the student general student body.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If they want to -- no-

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: I- if they want to try out or d- or aud- audition for one of those competitive activities, they know in advance that one of the regulations i- i- i- is to be subjected...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to drug testing.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: what you're arguing is is is a justification for the present policy that you've got, and I understand your position there.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: in.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Well, I think there is a an a reasonably good argument for that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, I I realize...

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: even if we utilize the balancing test that was utilized in Vernonia, I I think there is a...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: really good argument...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: then the whole notion of special need has has, more or less, evaporated.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I suppose the...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: distinction that you draw is based on the fact that you have a sanction for the people that you apply it to.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: E- exactly, Your Honor, and and that really is our position because...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: we c- have...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to educate the...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: you impose is you just can't play in the band or or cheerlead or whatever it is...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: they want to do.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: You can remain in the class.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: So, you have a special...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: burden if you want to engage in extracurricular activities.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, how about...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: taking elective classes, classes that the State doesn't require you to take, but some students might like to take?

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, we had to draw a line somewhere, and and e- even though elective...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: classes are a choice...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: the line that you would draw as a question of the school's policy, but you were saying i- at least you see a problem with testing everyone because people are compelled to go to school and so th- that's why you resisted drawing the line there.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: My question is if...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: you're doing a voluntary, then why don't you say as well, you volunteer for you're taking a certain course, this is elective course.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Well well, elective courses are really mandatory, Your Honor, in many cases.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Except for that, it'd be a good idea, though, wouldn't it?

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Ye- i- Yes Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What I thought you'd s-

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: if we could fashion a way to do it, I believe the majority of school boards would be behind it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: extracurricular --

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Is there any other sanction other than i- oth- other than ineligibility for extracurricular extracurricular activities? Is there any other sanction imposed on a student who is found to've been using drugs?

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: No no, Your Honor, and in fact, that only happens in this policy under the third time...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: So, really the student could say, well, the worst is a- if I g- go out for ecert- extracurricular activities and get caught, it'll be it'll be back where if I hadn't gone out at all.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: gets back wh- to square one.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: W- well, I think g- I think children who engage in these activities really want to compete.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Could you,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: for...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: that reason, adopt as a as one of your sanctions or a supplementary sanction simply publicity that the person had been caught? Anybody who fails the drug test will have his name and photograph posted on the bulletin board.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I I I think it would be...

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: very cruel, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: As to whether...

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: so whether it is constitutional or not I we're we're not in in in the job of disciplining students.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Very well, Ms. Meoli.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Suppose you've had a s- s- hard statistical evidence to show that the kids that go out for the extracurricular activities are the ones that d- really do not use drugs, that the high drug user population is in those that don't engage in the activities.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: I I think in a case like that, it still might be appropriate for the school district to test the students involved in extracurricular activities, and I think that is because, as this Court has emphasized on a number of occasions, by making the the the testing program applicable only to an avoidable activity, the the school district would be ensuring the reasonableness of the program under the Fourth...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Amendment.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: there're some we we had an amicus brief by some pediatrics association and so forth that pointed out that students that engage in these extracurricular activities are, indeed, the least likely to be involved in drug use.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well a co-

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: It's so counterintuitive, isn't it?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: A couple of points in response to that, Justice O'Connor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: t- school wide.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: face of a school district that h- certifying to the Federal Government they don't have a drug problem.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, I don't think it's that odd, Justice O'Connor.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, if alcohol's the problem, why don't they address that? This is just I I I mean, i- it's structured in a way to do very little good it seems...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to me.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: do you think any school in the country doesn't have a drug problem?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: I would be surprised to find a school district that didn't.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Can Can you tell me...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Skinner and Von Raab, we imposed or we permitted the imposition of drug testing on the basis of a nationwide problem, didn't we, without without showing that the particular entity in question shared in that national problem?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Scalia.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, d- do you...

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: drug counseling, I'm not sure they want to ask him w- what groups are...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: you in.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: May I ask?

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: What is your view on the v- on the validity of a a school wide drug testing program?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Justice Stevens, we think such a program would be constitutional, but we think the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Your view differs...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: from the petitioner's then.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: You We We do have that difference, but we think a program like this is constitutional for three reasons.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Second, as I...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: they agree to it.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Two points, Justice Souter.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But it it doesn't...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: you a question that that is concern concerns me about the difference between this case and Vernonia? If we if we look at what people might expect in the real world, where people know that athletes, professional athletes, Olympic athletes, athletes are tested for drugs, but people who are just everyday people aren't.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, I I mean, I th- I think i- w- one fact is that I think it is increasingly becoming true that these these sorts of policies are in in society as a whole.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: recognize...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: y- you would make and I think you've said this the same argument if they had decided to have a universal drug testing policy in the school.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: I I would, Justice Souter.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: reasonableness.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: th- that point, I I the the so called special need has become virtually a universal need.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: I I would like to say two things in response to that.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: And and the special need would be a universal need in every school in the United States.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: I I I do think it would properly recognize that the school context is different, and that school boards have a freer hand in testing their students than the Government has testing either employees or it's...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: citizens at large.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: that is the case, what is the danger in the school case, comparable to the to the railroad worker's danger or the the the customs officer's danger? What is the danger as distinct from simply the desire to deter illegal drug use?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: I think the danger is this.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But in fr-

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: getting young people used to a drug culture.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: I agree with that, Justice Scalia, and I would I would add that this is not the only context where where where the Federal Government has recognized that difference.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: hardly a revelation that the Government is concerned about what drugs do to our culture.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: A- Absolutely, and I think it's...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: perfectly...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: rocket science, is it?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: No, and I and I don't think it's rocket science also to say that that that concern is particularly acute with respect to the youngest and most vulnerable members of society.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Clement.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court Opposing counsel said we have to draw the line somewhere, and the way the way to draw the line in this case is to keep in mind the core principle that individualized reasonable suspicion is the standard for school searches set forth in TLO.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the core of TLO...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: individualized suspicion in Vernonia.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: There there was not, and Vernonia is...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, so...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: so there goes your principle.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Th- Vernonia is the exception to the TLO rule.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Can have medical metal detectors in schools where they're afraid the children have guns?

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Yes, they can.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Now, there's no individualized suspicion there, is...

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: No, there's not,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and the reason...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: throat swabs if you feel that there is a contagious disease?

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Throat swabs if you think there's a contagious disease.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I was trying to f- well, that's...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: what I I raised that because, of course, everyone is making a criminal law analogy,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: but nobody's arrested here.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Nobody is arrested.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: and and I just wonder.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well, unless the Court stands ready to overrule TLO, I believe it is the standard.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: observa-

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: it's absolutely clear there isn't individualized suspicion, so you win automatically if that's the test, I would think.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: course, it wasn't in Vernonia.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Of of course.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: being drug tested.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: presumably a year round thing in a way that football is not.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: No, it's not, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Are are you saying that, for instance, choir in T- Tecumseh is only in the fall?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The competi-

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: in the winter or only in the spring?

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, in terms of the competitions, they are.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: are of concern to the school.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: give me an ou- Wh- Wh- Wha- Which Which season of the four season is for band in Tecumseh?

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: I i- I I honestly don't know what months of the year they compete in, and and...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: They can't play for all these four seasons, I guess. But  let let...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: me let me ask y- ask you this.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Mhm.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: and that's what's happening here.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well, Justice Kennedy, you're absolutely right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I need to p-

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: me if a school is better than other school districts s- s- insofar as drug use, they have less drug use, that they're maybe entitled to keep it that way.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: to say not.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well, there there're couple of things.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But they have a record here that, of course, suggests if you want to know if drugs are going on in your school, you ask the kids.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I don't really see how that de- re- I mean, you know, you might be able to drive a millimeter of light between that and Vernonia.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, J-

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: and try, but I I can't I I mean, I I I they did what I would have done.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: and they get a positive response when it's a question of drugs.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Justice Breyer, I I think you're you're right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Why? Now, I...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Of course if I read the cases, as I do, and they logically require you to win, that's the end.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But just in case it's open I would I would like to know why.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Two, we don't want to put the student, although we'd really like to, to the choice of school versus drug testing.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well, I i- I I mean, I I think what's perhaps behind your question is a little bit of what's the law and why is the law that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: way and a  little bit of why the policy...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: behind the question is that I think undoubtedly you're right, that this is a slight expansion of Vernonia.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: you to...

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Okay, fair enough.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: last sentence on that page...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: this argument counsel.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well, it...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: it it...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And what what I miss in your argument is any recognition of the fact that we are dealing here with minors.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Let let me, l- l- Let let let me let me do two things, if I may, Justice Scalia.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: her privacy as part of that?

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: proposition that the only valid basis for conducting drug searches is to is to enable the school to maintain discipline.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: No, no.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: long as you have a bunch of druggies who are or- or a orderly in class, the school can take no action.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Is...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: is that what you want us to...

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: A- A-

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Absolutely...

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: about in loco parentis.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: made in Vernonia, and it does go across the board, and that was the statement that drug testing on suspicion, which is the alternative, because in life, it's everything as compared to what.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: and diverts the teachers...

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: who are engaged in that exercise from the job of teaching to being a police officer.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg, there's a good answer to that question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: kids.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: but the in loco parentis argument works against you there too because in loco parentis also indicates that all children are forced to go to this school.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: And we have to accept that a majority of the parents want to make sure that those children are in a drug free atmosphere.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: In lo-

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: And they have they and and you you seem to me to be disparaging about police dogs and locker locker searches, maybe that's what you're going to challenge next.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Justice...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: rights of other children...

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: who want to go to a school which is drug free, if they can.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Absolutely,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Justice Kennedy.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: me that's part of in loco parentis too because those students are required to go to the school just as as the drug user is.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: A- y- y- you're absolutely right about that, and please don't hear me to be disparaging of those other tools because those are tools that do not include a blanket intrusive search, which is the principle that's at issue here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, why does it...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: make any difference why you applaud it or not?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Oh, it probably doesn't. It probably doesn't.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: It d- it does.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: school...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: saying that the in loco parentis principle does not apply in the case of a school if a minority of parents object to what the school is doing?

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: That's not my...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, I...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: thought that was what you were saying a moment ago?

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Let m- let me please try to be more clear about that.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, how how did...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Hazel-

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: how does Hazelwood fit that description?

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well, because of the disruption that could be caused by the sch- by the school paper articles.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: disruption...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: think the reason in Hazelwood w- was ju- wh- the discipline was justified was i- i- caused disruption, but because when you're learning in school, you follow the instructions of the teacher.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well, and and also the form issue.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You did, yeah.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: I did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the student...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: w- sa- say again why you you think the Vernonia situation, with respect to this point, is different than the Tecumseh.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the drug use.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: that i- questions been battered b- batted back and forth that comment...

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Mhm.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: y- I you're y- you're you're not saying, are you, that unless the school has the sort of discipline problem they had in Vernonia, it can't do this?

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: No, I'm not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: don't...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: on the drug test? If you had a choice, in terms of which is the I understand you think this is clearly unconstitutional.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: I'm not really sure that one can can r- can rank more and less constitutional.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Justice Stevens.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Scalia g- would would respond by saying, well, everybody knows there's a potential problem in every school in the country, and that's enough.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And why isn't that an effective argument?

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: it's not enough because what that would say is that i- i- t- it is really that the balancing test behind the Fourth Amendment reasonableness requirement becomes one where there's no weight on one side of the of the balance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: vague, so let me Okay.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: they've come in with a brief on the other side and said, you know, despite the fact that we're spending X billion dollars, two-thirds of which goes on the supply side for interdiction...

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: demand has stayed constant among teenagers, and it's about twenty to thirty percent of all of the children in these high schools who take drugs.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: would...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: be constitutional, to say here's a particular problem, twenty to thirty percent of the kids are taking drugs, and we want to come in with a solution that's tailored to that?

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well, it's you know, the number is probably, according to Mr. Clement, is more like fifty percent, and and that fifty percent of of the kids in the high...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: schools are using drugs.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: than I thought...

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: And...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: then wh-

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: wha- if that if...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: that's...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: fine, then then then i- then there is the problem you talked about, which I just heard you say.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well, if it's fifty percent nationally, this school in its reports to the Federal Government says that its school wide drug use based on its own surveys, which is where the national data comes from, surveys, is five percent.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: five percent. So...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: may- maybe they exaggerated in that report.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: He didn't put numbers on it.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Right, he he did...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: not.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: used the word serious problem.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: o- in your opinion a school that did find that it was within, let's say, half the average, say twenty percent or...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: twenty-five...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: percent, and they did feel that a significant number of their extracurricular activity students were involved in that, if if they've th- heard that through hearsay or any other way that was reasonably plausible for...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: policy makers,

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: they could then do this in your opinion.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: No, they could not because there's still is a need for a safety rationale.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: non-athletes. There's...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: rationale with golfers in Vernonia?

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well, the the safety rationale i- i- I mean, certainly the Court in Vernonia didn't talk about golfers one way or...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: or another, but...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: you're saying that Vernonia was based on a safety...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: ration-

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Its testing included golfers.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well, in Vernonia, the Court talked at some length about the safety rationale.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: choir.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: overdose?

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Death from overdose is certainly a...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: concern, but there's no...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: life and death is is i- is is really not involved in in in in in the fight against drugs?

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: It it absolutely is, Justice Scalia, and whe- and...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Let's not...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: minimize that.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: I I don't mean to, and where there is evidence of drug use among a group of students,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: then I th-

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: a- a- as far as as far as the extent of the drug use is concerned, th- this is an elected school board, isn't it?

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: It is.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: W- w- why why should I trust your assessment of how serious the drug problem is and what measures are reasonable to counter that that seriousness over the assessment of of the local citizens who who elect their school board, and their school board says we have a big enough problem that we want to use this draconian measure?

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well, I think if we listen to the school board and to the school administration, who themselves are even closer to that problem, what they say is, we've identified enough of a problem that initially we want to test our athletes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: problem. We don't have any...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: the school district said, we're going to have two schools and you can go to either one.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: One,

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: they don't have dogs or or tests or anything else.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: And and and and the other school and the other school is they have m- mandatory testing for everybody.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: I don't think so because I think even by the nature of that hypothetical, you're it it presupposes one of the schools is going to be vastly inferior on a number of grounds.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Vast experience why?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: E- exc-

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: because there're drug users there.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well, I I think n- no I don't think that's the reason why, I...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: No...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: parent, no parent would would send the the child to the first school that I suggested, other than perhaps your client wants to go there.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Well, sh- she absolutely would not, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: would attest to that.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: doesn't don't magnet schools have rules for uniforms and s- and so forth that that are they're completely optional? A school district could have two schools, one with one with no testing, the other with testing.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: I I think uniforms are a world apart.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: not an intrusive blanket search. Exactly. It it is.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: What what do you th- what do you say just frankly to the argument, forget individualized need, forget special need entirely, forget suspicion.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: I I think the answer to the argument is the Fourth Amendment turns on reasonableness.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That doesn't answer the...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's the question. Yeah.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: I see that my time is up.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Ex- yes.

turns.name: GRAHAM A. BOYD
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Thank thank you,

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Mr. Boyd Ms. Meoli, you have one minute remaining.

turns.name: LINDA M. MEOLI
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, if there are no further questions, I'll waive the remainder of my time.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Very well.
